SOUTH-EAST UNIVERSITY

Department of Computer Science



CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications

Module Handbook — Academic Year 2024/2025

1. Module Overview

Detail

Information

Module Code

CSC302

Module Title

Artificial Intelligence & Applications

Level

Year 3 Undergraduate (Level 6)

Credit Units

15 credits

Contact Hours

36 hours (24 lectures + 12 lab sessions)

Self-Study Hours

114 hours

Total Learning Hours

150 hours

Module Coordinator

Dr Amaka Obi

Email

a.obi@seu.edu.ng

Office

Block C, Room 214

Office Hours

Tuesdays 10:00–12:00 and Thursdays 14:00–16:00

2. What This Module Is About

CSC302 introduces the foundational theories, techniques, and practical applications of Artificial Intelligence. The module moves from classical search and knowledge representation through to modern machine learning and neural networks, ending with a serious look at the ethical questions that come with deploying AI in the real world.

Students will implement AI algorithms in Python across twelve weeks of lab work, and will finish the module with a group project that builds and evaluates a small AI-based system. The module is intended to prepare students for advanced study in AI or machine learning, and for roles in software development, research, and technology consultancy.

3. Prerequisites

Before enrolling in CSC302 you must have completed CSC201 (Data Structures and Algorithms), CSC202 (Programming in Python), and MAT201 (Probability and Statistics for Computing). If you have not completed these modules, you need written permission from the Module Coordinator before you can register.

4. Learning Outcomes

By the end of the module, you should be able to:

Explain the core concepts, history, and scope of AI and its main subfields.

Implement and evaluate classical search algorithms, including uninformed and informed approaches.

Represent knowledge using logic and rules, and apply reasoning to solve defined problems.

Apply supervised and unsupervised machine learning techniques to real datasets in Python.

Describe the principles and applications of natural language processing and computer vision.

Critically evaluate the ethical, social, and legal implications of deploying AI systems.

Design and present a small AI-based solution, with documented justification of your design decisions.



5. Module Content and Weekly Schedule

Week

Topic

Outcome(s)

1

Introduction to AI: history, definitions, types of AI, intelligent agents

LO1

2

Problem-solving and search: uninformed search (BFS, DFS, UCS)

LO2

3

Informed search: heuristics, A*, greedy best-first

LO2

4

Knowledge representation: propositional and first-order logic

LO3

5

Expert systems and rule-based reasoning

LO3

6

Introduction to machine learning: supervised vs unsupervised

LO4

7

Supervised learning: linear regression, decision trees, SVM

LO4

8

Unsupervised learning: K-means clustering, PCA

LO4

9

Neural networks and deep learning fundamentals

LO4

10

Natural language processing: tokenisation, sentiment analysis, LLMs

LO5

11

Computer vision: image classification, CNNs

LO5

12

AI ethics, bias, fairness, transparency, and accountability

LO6

13–14

Project workshops and peer review

LO7

15

Revision week — no new content

All



6. Assessment

Component

Type

Weight

Due

CW01

Individual Written Report

40%

Week 8, Friday 17:00

CW02

Group AI Implementation Project

40%

Week 14, Friday 17:00

EX01

Written Examination (2 hours)

20%

Examination period, Weeks 16–17

6. Assessment

Component

Type

Weight

Due

CW01

Individual Written Report

40%

Week 8, Friday 17:00

CW02

Group AI Implementation Project

40%

Week 14, Friday 17:00

EX01

Written Examination (2 hours)

20%

Examination period, Weeks 16–17



The pass mark is 40% overall. You must also achieve at least 35% in each individual component. Students who fail the module are entitled to one resit attempt; resit marks are capped at 40%.

7. Recommended Reading

Core Textbooks

Russell, S. and Norvig, P. (2021) Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach, 4th edn. Hoboken: Pearson. This is the primary text for the module and five copies are available in the SEU library.

Géron, A. (2022) Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow, 3rd edn. Sebastopol: O'Reilly. Available online through the SEU library portal.

Supplementary Reading

Mitchell, M. (2019) Artificial Intelligence: A Guide for Thinking Humans. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux.

Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y. and Courville, A. (2016) Deep Learning. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. The full text is freely available at deeplearningbook.org.

8. How the Module Runs

Lectures run for two hours each Monday morning (09:00–11:00, Lecture Hall B2). They introduce the week's concepts through worked examples and discussion. Slides are posted on the VLE within 24 hours of each session. Lab sessions (Thursdays, 14:00–15:00, CS Lab 3) give you supervised time to implement what you've covered in lectures using Python and Google Colab. You're expected to attempt the pre-lab exercises before you arrive.

Outside of contact time, you should expect to spend around eight hours per week on reading, coursework preparation, and revision. Office hours with Dr Obi run on Tuesdays 10:00–12:00 and Thursdays 14:00–16:00 in Block C, Room 214. You can also email to arrange a specific appointment.

9. Academic Integrity

All submitted work must be your own — or, for CW02, your group's. SEU uses Turnitin for written submissions and may use manual code comparison for coding work. Plagiarism, contract cheating, or using AI tools to generate submitted work without declaring it will result in a mark of zero and may be referred for formal disciplinary action.

If you are unsure whether a particular use of an AI tool is acceptable for a given assessment, ask the Module Coordinator in writing before you submit. It is much easier to clarify in advance than to deal with an integrity query afterwards.

10. Attendance

You are expected to attend all scheduled sessions. If you miss a lecture or lab, catching up is your responsibility using the materials posted on VLE. Students whose attendance falls below 70% without a documented reason may be excluded from the examination.

11. Student Support

The Departmental Student Office (Block A, Room 101) is open Monday to Friday, 08:30–16:30 and handles all administrative matters. The SEU Library offers 24/7 online access and is open physically Monday to Saturday, 08:00–20:00. Students who need learning adjustments — such as extra time in exams — should register with the Disability and Inclusion Office before the end of Week 3.

12. Feedback

Written feedback on CW01 and CW02 will be returned within 15 working days of each submission deadline. Aggregate examination feedback will be posted on the VLE within four weeks of results release. If you want to discuss your marks in person, you may request a feedback meeting with the Module Coordinator within two weeks of receiving your results.

Version 1.0 — Published September 2024 — Next review August 2026 — South-East University

SOUTH-EAST UNIVERSITY

Department of Computer Science



CW01 — Individual Written Report

CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications — Assessment Brief 2024/2025

1. At a Glance

Detail

Information

Assessment Code

CW01

Type

Individual Written Report

Weight

40% of module total

Word Limit

2,500 words (±10%) — excludes references, figures, and appendices

Deadline

Friday, 8 November 2024 at 17:00 WAT (Week 8)

Submission

Online via SEU VLE: Turnitin portal

File Format

PDF only (.pdf)

File Name

CW01_[StudentNumber]_CSC302.pdf — e.g. CW01_2024001_CSC302.pdf

Feedback Returned

Within 15 working days of the deadline



2. The Task

Write an individual analytical report on the following question:



"Evaluate the strengths and limitations of machine learning approaches compared to rule-based AI systems, with reference to a specific real-world application domain of your choice."



Your report needs to do four things well. First, define and distinguish machine learning and rule-based AI systems using precise technical definitions. Second, pick one real-world application domain — something specific like healthcare diagnostics, fraud detection, or agricultural monitoring — and explain clearly why AI is relevant there. Third, compare the two approaches within that domain with reference to at least six academic sources, weighing up genuine strengths and limitations rather than just listing features. Fourth, conclude with a justified recommendation on which approach (or combination) suits your domain, acknowledging the trade-offs involved.

Every report must also include at least one paragraph on the ethical implications of deploying AI in your chosen domain. This is assessed directly and needs to go beyond surface-level observations about bias or privacy.




3. Learning Outcomes Assessed

This coursework assesses LO1 (understanding of AI concepts and scope), LO4 (machine learning techniques), and LO6 (critical evaluation of ethical and social implications).

4. Marking Criteria

Criterion

Marks

Understanding of AI concepts: accuracy and depth of definitions; correct use of technical terminology

20

Critical analysis and comparison: quality of argument; use of evidence; balance across both approaches

30

Application to chosen domain: relevance and specificity; domain-grounded recommendation

20

Ethical discussion: identification and analysis of relevant issues; goes beyond surface-level

15

Academic referencing and writing: Harvard accuracy; academic register; structure and clarity

15

Total

100



5. How to Submit

Log into the SEU VLE at vle.seu.edu.ng, navigate to CSC302 → Assessments → CW01 Submission, and upload your PDF. After submitting, check your Turnitin similarity report — scores above 20% (excluding the reference list) will be reviewed. Download your submission receipt and keep it.

Only PDF files are accepted. Any other format will receive zero. The file must be named exactly as shown above. The file size limit is 10 MB.

6. Late Submission and Extensions

A penalty of five marks per working day is applied to late submissions, up to a maximum of five working days. Submissions more than five working days late receive zero unless an extension has been approved in advance.

Extension requests must be made before the deadline through the Departmental Student Office (Block A, Room 101 or studentoffice@seu.edu.ng), with supporting evidence attached. Self-certification is available for up to two working days once per semester, with no evidence required, but must be requested before the deadline. Extensions are not granted retrospectively except in exceptional documented circumstances.

7. Frequently Asked Questions

Can I choose any application domain?

Yes, as long as it is a real domain where AI is actively used or seriously proposed. Avoid vague choices like "technology" or "business." The stronger reports tend to pick something narrow enough to analyse in depth — for example, early diagnosis of diabetic retinopathy, or credit risk scoring in retail banking.

How many references do I need?

A minimum of six academic sources is required. Strong reports typically cite ten to fifteen sources, drawing on peer-reviewed journals and conference papers rather than relying on websites or textbooks alone.

Can I use AI writing tools?

Only for initial brainstorming. Any AI-assisted content must be declared in an appendix. Undeclared use will be treated as academic misconduct. When in doubt, ask the Module Coordinator in writing before you submit.



CW01 Assessment Brief v1.0 — CSC302 — South-East University — 2024/2025

SOUTH-EAST UNIVERSITY

Department of Computer Science



CW02 — Group AI Implementation Project

CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications — Assessment Brief 2024/2025

1. At a Glance

Detail

Information

Assessment Code

CW02

Type

Group Implementation Project (3–4 students)

Weight

40% of module total

Deadline

Friday, 20 December 2024 at 17:00 WAT (Week 14)

Group Registration Closes

Friday, 18 October 2024 at 17:00 (Week 5)

Deliverables

(1) Technical report PDF + (2) Code repository link + (3) 10-minute demo video

Report File Name

CW02_Group[Number]_CSC302.pdf — e.g. CW02_Group07_CSC302.pdf

Feedback Returned

Within 15 working days of the deadline



2. The Task

In groups of three or four, you will design, implement, and evaluate a small AI-based system that addresses a defined real-world problem. The project must incorporate at least one AI or machine learning technique covered in CSC302, and must be built in Python.

Your system needs to have a clear and specific problem statement, a working implementation with a trained or rule-based AI component, a user interface (command-line is acceptable; a web or GUI interface earns higher marks), and an evaluation using at least one dataset with appropriate performance metrics.

You may use any of the AI/ML techniques covered in the module: supervised or unsupervised learning, search algorithms, rule-based reasoning, or natural language processing. Standard Python libraries — scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, NLTK, spaCy — are all permitted.

3. Deliverables

Technical Report (PDF)

4,000 words (±10%), excluding code listings, figures, and references. Must include: problem statement and motivation, system design, implementation details, evaluation results, ethical reflection, and an individual contribution statement signed by all group members. File name: CW02_Group[Number]_CSC302.pdf.

Code Repository

A private GitHub or GitLab repository, with access shared with Dr Obi (a.obi@seu.edu.ng) before the deadline. The repository must include a README.md with setup instructions, run steps, and a list of dependencies. The code must run as described.

Demo Video

A screen-capture recording of no more than ten minutes, demonstrating the working system. All group members should speak at some point. Upload as an MP4 file (maximum 500 MB) via the VLE.

4. Marking Criteria

Criterion

Marks

Problem definition and motivation — clarity of problem; justification for AI/ML approach

10

System design and AI technique — correct application; design quality; justified choices

25

Implementation and code quality — working code; readability; appropriate use of libraries

25

Evaluation and results — appropriate metrics; honest interpretation; baseline or comparison

20

Ethical and critical reflection — limitations and real-world deployment concerns

10

Report quality and referencing — clarity; structure; Harvard referencing accuracy

10

Total

100



All group members normally receive the same mark. Where there is evidence of significantly unequal contribution, the Module Coordinator may hold a short individual viva and adjust marks accordingly.

5. Group Registration

Groups must be registered on the VLE by the end of Week 5 (Friday, 18 October 2024, 17:00). Nominate one member as Group Lead, who will be responsible for the final submission. Any student not in a registered group after this date will be assigned randomly by the Module Coordinator.

6. Late Submission and Extensions

The same penalty applies as for CW01: five marks per working day, up to five days, then zero. Extensions apply to the whole group — individual members cannot extend independently. If a group member withdraws from the module, the remaining members should contact the Module Coordinator as soon as possible and no later than Week 12.

7. Academic Integrity

Code from tutorials or open-source repositories must be acknowledged in your comments and in the report. AI coding tools such as GitHub Copilot may be used but must be declared. Each group member is expected to understand all parts of the submission; undeclared use or falsifying the contribution statement constitutes academic misconduct.



CW02 Assessment Brief v1.0 — CSC302 — South-East University — 2024/2025

SOUTH-EAST UNIVERSITY

Department of Computer Science



Marking Rubric — CW01 and CW02

CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications — 2024/2025

This document describes what markers are looking for at each performance level. Reading it carefully before you write will help you understand what separates a strong submission from a weak one. The grade scale at the end of this document applies to all CSC302 assessments.

CW01 — Individual Written Report

Criterion 1: Understanding of AI Concepts (20 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

17–20 — Excellent

Definitions are precise and technically accurate throughout. The student demonstrates a deep understanding of both approaches, uses correct terminology consistently, and draws on authoritative sources to support their explanations.

13–16 — Good

Definitions are mostly accurate with minor technical gaps. Terminology is largely correct. The distinction between approaches is clear but lacks depth in places.

9–12 — Satisfactory

Basic definitions are provided but contain some inaccuracies or oversimplifications. There is some confusion between related terms and only partial distinction between the two approaches.

5–8 — Weak

Definitions are superficial, inaccurate, or missing in places. Limited evidence of understanding AI concepts beyond surface-level description.

0–4 — Very Poor

Little to no demonstration of understanding. Fundamental misconceptions are present.



Criterion 2: Critical Analysis and Comparison (30 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

25–30 — Excellent

Sophisticated, balanced comparison with well-evidenced arguments. Strengths and limitations of both approaches are genuinely analysed rather than listed. Evidence from academic sources is integrated into the argument, not just cited. Counter-arguments are acknowledged.

19–24 — Good

Solid comparison with clear arguments. Most strengths and limitations are covered. Sources are used but occasionally dropped in without full integration into the argument.

13–18 — Satisfactory

Reasonable comparison but lacking balance — may focus more heavily on one approach. Arguments are partly supported by evidence but not consistently.

7–12 — Weak

Largely descriptive rather than analytical. Few sources used, or used without meaningful engagement.

0–6 — Very Poor

No meaningful comparison. Superficial or incorrect statements. No evidence-based reasoning.



Criterion 3: Application to Chosen Domain (20 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

17–20 — Excellent

Domain is well chosen and clearly justified. Analysis engages specifically with how the domain's requirements and constraints shape the comparison. The recommendation is convincingly linked to domain-specific factors.

13–16 — Good

Domain is appropriate and reasonably justified. Most analysis is domain-specific. Recommendation is present and clear but could be more tightly justified.

9–12 — Satisfactory

Domain is identified but the analysis remains largely generic. Limited engagement with what makes the domain distinctive.

5–8 — Weak

Domain choice is unclear or the justification is absent. Analysis does not engage with domain specifics.

0–4 — Very Poor

No clear domain or an entirely irrelevant one. Analysis is generic throughout.



Criterion 4: Ethical Discussion (15 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

13–15 — Excellent

Substantive ethical discussion that is linked to the specific domain and AI approach chosen. Engages with concepts such as bias, fairness, transparency, accountability, or unintended consequences. References relevant literature or frameworks.

10–12 — Good

Ethical issues identified and explained, mostly domain-relevant. Limited engagement with frameworks or external sources.

6–9 — Satisfactory

Ethical issues mentioned but discussion is superficial or generic. Does not go beyond obvious observations.

3–5 — Weak

Very brief ethical mention that is not integrated into the wider argument.

0–2 — Very Poor

Absent or irrelevant.



Criterion 5: Academic Referencing and Writing (15 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

13–15 — Excellent

Harvard referencing applied accurately and consistently. Writing is clear, well-structured, and at an appropriate academic register. The argument flows logically from introduction through to conclusion.

10–12 — Good

Referencing mostly correct with minor errors. Writing is clear with occasional lapses in academic style. Structure is mostly effective.

6–9 — Satisfactory

Noticeable referencing errors. Writing is readable but informal in places. Structure could be improved.

3–5 — Weak

Frequent referencing errors. Writing is unclear or informal in significant portions.

0–2 — Very Poor

No referencing or entirely incorrect. Writing is difficult to follow.

Criterion 5: Academic Referencing and Writing (15 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

13–15 — Excellent

Harvard referencing applied accurately and consistently. Writing is clear, well-structured, and at an appropriate academic register. The argument flows logically from introduction through to conclusion.

10–12 — Good

Referencing mostly correct with minor errors. Writing is clear with occasional lapses in academic style. Structure is mostly effective.

6–9 — Satisfactory

Noticeable referencing errors. Writing is readable but informal in places. Structure could be improved.

3–5 — Weak

Frequent referencing errors. Writing is unclear or informal in significant portions.

0–2 — Very Poor

No referencing or entirely incorrect. Writing is difficult to follow.



CW02 — Group AI Implementation Project

Criterion 1: Problem Definition and Motivation (10 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

9–10

Problem is specific, clearly stated, and well justified as an AI/ML task. Scope is realistic given a student project.

7–8

Problem is clear and the AI justification is reasonable. Scope is mostly appropriate.

5–6

Problem is vague or too broad. AI justification is weak or generic.

0–4

No clear problem definition or no justification for using AI.



Criterion 2: System Design and AI Technique (25 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

21–25

Correct and appropriate application of the chosen AI/ML technique. Design is clearly documented with justified decisions. Architecture or design description is easy to follow.

16–20

Technique correctly applied. Design is documented but with some gaps. Most choices are justified.

11–15

Technique applied with noticeable errors or partial misapplication. Design documentation is incomplete.

0–10

Incorrect or absent technique. No meaningful design documentation.



Criterion 3: Implementation and Code Quality (25 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

21–25

Code runs correctly. It is well structured, readable, and appropriately commented. Good use of libraries. Modular design is evident.

16–20

Code largely works. Mostly readable with some structural issues or limited commenting.

11–15

Code partially works. Limited readability or structure. Some missing or broken components.

0–10

Code does not run, has significant bugs, or is mostly absent.



Criterion 4: Evaluation and Results (20 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

17–20

Appropriate metrics used and correctly calculated. Results interpreted honestly, including weaknesses. Baseline or comparison provided.

13–16

Appropriate metrics. Mostly correct interpretation. Limited comparison.

9–12

Some evaluation present but metrics may be inappropriate or results misinterpreted.

0–8

Minimal evaluation or entirely absent.



Criterion 5: Ethical and Critical Reflection (10 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

9–10

Substantive reflection on limitations and ethical risks specific to the system built. Engages with real deployment concerns, not just theoretical ones.

7–8

Good reflection on limitations. Ethical discussion is present but slightly generic.

0–6

Surface-level or absent.



Criterion 6: Report Quality and Referencing (10 marks)

Mark Range

What this looks like

9–10

Well-written and clearly structured. Accurate Harvard referencing throughout.

7–8

Mostly clear writing and structure. Minor referencing errors.

0–6

Structural issues, unclear writing, or frequent referencing errors.



Grade Scale

Grade

Mark Range

Classification

A

70–100%

First Class

B

60–69%

Upper Second (2:1)

C

50–59%

Lower Second (2:2)

D

40–49%

Third Class (Pass)

F

0–39%

Marking Rubric v1.0 — CSC302 — South-East University — 2024/2025

SOUTH-EAST UNIVERSITY

Department of Computer Science



Late Submission and Extension Policy

CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications — 2024/2025

This document sets out the rules for late submission and the process for requesting extensions. These rules apply to both CW01 and CW02 and are consistent with the South-East University Academic Regulations (2024/2025). Where there is any conflict, the university regulations take precedence.

1. Deadlines

Assessment

Deadline

CW01 — Individual Report

Friday, 8 November 2024 at 17:00 WAT

CW02 — Group Project

Friday, 20 December 2024 at 17:00 WAT



The VLE submission portal closes automatically at the stated time. Submitting on time is your responsibility. Do not leave it to the last hour — technical problems close to a deadline do not automatically grant extensions.

2. Late Submission Penalty

How Late

Penalty Applied

Up to 1 working day

5 marks deducted from the achieved mark

1–2 working days

10 marks deducted

2–3 working days

15 marks deducted

3–4 working days

20 marks deducted

4–5 working days

25 marks deducted

More than 5 working days

Zero (0) awarded regardless of mark



Working days are Monday to Friday, excluding public holidays and university closure days. To give an example: if a student submits CW01 at 09:00 on Monday when the deadline was Friday at 17:00, that is one working day late. If their report would have received 62%, the penalty reduces it to 57%.

3. Extensions

What Qualifies

Extensions are available for genuinely unforeseen and unpreventable circumstances that significantly disrupted your ability to complete the work on time. Circumstances that normally qualify include serious personal illness with medical documentation, the serious illness or death of a close family member, and significant personal crises backed by evidence.

Circumstances that do not normally qualify include poor time management, technical problems with your own device (always keep backups and use university computers if needed), mild illness such as a cold, work or employment commitments, and simply forgetting the deadline. Disagreements with group members for CW02 are also not ordinarily grounds for an extension, though they may be escalated separately — see Section 5.

How to Request

Contact the Departmental Student Office (Block A, Room 101 or studentoffice@seu.edu.ng) before the original deadline wherever possible. Complete the Extension Request Form, which is available at the Student Office and on the VLE. Attach supporting evidence. The Student Office will inform the Module Coordinator, who will respond within two working days. You will receive email confirmation of the outcome and any revised deadline.

Self-Certification (Short Extension)

For minor unforeseen circumstances, you can self-certify for up to two extra working days without providing documentation. This option is available once per semester per student and must be requested before the original deadline through the Student Office. It cannot be combined with a longer extension for the same assessment.

Retrospective Requests

Extensions requested after the deadline has passed are only considered in exceptional circumstances, such as hospitalisation that prevented any communication. Contact the Student Office as soon as you are able.

4. Fit to Sit

By submitting coursework or sitting an examination, you are declaring yourself fit to do so at that time. If you later raise your health as a reason for poor performance, retrospective consideration is unlikely unless there are documented exceptional circumstances that prevented you from raising it beforehand.

5. Group Work — CW02

Extensions for CW02 are granted to the whole group; individual members cannot hold different deadlines. If a group member withdraws from the module or becomes significantly unavailable, the remaining members should raise this with the Module Coordinator before Week 12. Do not leave it until the submission deadline.

6. Examination

If you miss EX01 due to extenuating circumstances, contact the Departmental Student Office within three working days of the exam date. You may be entitled to a sit in the next available sitting, subject to university-level approval. Contact the Student Office, not the Module Coordinator, for missed examination queries.

7. Appeals

If you believe a penalty has been applied incorrectly or that an extension decision was procedurally unfair, you may appeal in writing to the Head of Department within ten working days. Appeals must be based on procedural grounds and not simply disagreement with the outcome.

8. Key Contacts

Contact

Role

Details

Dr Amaka Obi

Module Coordinator

a.obi@seu.edu.ng — Block C, Room 214

Departmental Student Office

Extensions and forms

studentoffice@seu.edu.ng — Block A, Room 101

Reading Week Note

There are no lectures or lab sessions during Reading Week (28 October – 1 November). This time should be used to catch up on any missed material and to make substantial progress on CW01, which is due in Week 8.

Resits

Students who fail CSC302 and are eligible for a resit will be notified by email by 28 February 2025. Resit deadlines are typically in May or June 2025. Resit marks are capped at 40%.

Frequently Asked Questions

CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications — 2024/2025

This FAQ answers the questions students most commonly ask about CSC302. It draws on the official module documents, so the answers here are consistent with the handbook, assessment briefs, and policies. If your question is not answered here, contact Dr Obi or the Departmental Student Office.

Assessments and Deadlines

What are the deadlines for CW01 and CW02?

CW01 (Individual Report) is due on Friday, 8 November 2024 at 17:00 WAT. CW02 (Group Project) is due on Friday, 20 December 2024 at 17:00 WAT. Both are submitted online via the SEU VLE.

What is the word limit for CW01?

The word limit is 2,500 words with a 10% tolerance, meaning your report should fall between 2,250 and 2,750 words. References, figures, tables, and appendices are excluded from this count.

What is the word limit for CW02?

The technical report for CW02 has a word limit of 4,000 words (±10%), so between 3,600 and 4,400 words. Code listings, figures, and references are excluded.

What file format must I use for CW01?

PDF only. Word (.docx) or any other format will not be accepted and will receive a mark of zero. Convert your document before uploading.

What should the file name of my CW01 submission be?

It must follow this format exactly: CW01_[YourStudentNumber]_CSC302.pdf. For example: CW01_2024001_CSC302.pdf. Incorrect file names can delay marking.

What is the late penalty?

Five marks per working day are deducted for every day you submit after the deadline, up to a maximum of five working days. After that, the mark is zero regardless of quality. Working days are Monday to Friday, excluding public holidays.

What if I miss the deadline entirely?

If you are more than five working days late without an approved extension, your mark will be zero. If you have genuine extenuating circumstances, contact the Departmental Student Office before the deadline wherever possible.

How do I request an extension?

Contact the Departmental Student Office at studentoffice@seu.edu.ng or visit Block A, Room 101, before the deadline. Complete the Extension Request Form and attach supporting evidence. Self-certification is available for up to two working days once per semester and requires no evidence, but must be requested before the deadline.

Can I request an extension after the deadline has passed?

Only in exceptional circumstances such as hospitalisation that prevented any communication. Contact the Student Office as soon as you are able.

What is the pass mark?

The overall pass mark for the module is 40%. You must also achieve at least 35% in each individual component. Falling below 35% in any component may result in a module fail even if your overall average is above 40%.

Submission Process

Where do I submit my coursework?

All coursework is submitted online via the SEU VLE at vle.seu.edu.ng. Go to CSC302 → Assessments → the relevant submission portal. Do not email coursework to the Module Coordinator.

Can I submit by email?

No. Emailed submissions are not accepted. All work must go through the VLE Turnitin portal. If you experience technical problems near the deadline, contact the IT Helpdesk immediately and copy in the Student Office.

How do I know my submission was received?

After submitting via Turnitin, you will receive an email receipt to your SEU student email address. Download and save it as proof.

Can I resubmit before the deadline?

Yes. You can resubmit as many times as you like before the deadline; only the final version will be marked. After the deadline, resubmission is not possible unless you have an approved extension.

What is the Turnitin similarity limit?

There is no automatic block on submission, but similarity scores above 20% (excluding the reference list) will be reviewed manually. High similarity without proper quotation marks will be treated as potential plagiarism.

CW02 Group Project

How are groups formed?

You form your own group of three to four students. Groups must be registered on the VLE by the end of Week 5 (Friday, 18 October 2024, 17:00). Any student not in a registered group by this date will be assigned to a group by the Module Coordinator.

What if a group member stops contributing?

Raise this with Dr Obi as early as possible and no later than Week 12.

Yes. You can resubmit as many times as you like before the deadline; only the final version will be marked. After the deadline, resubmission is not possible unless you have an approved extension.

What is the Turnitin similarity limit?

There is no automatic block on submission, but similarity scores above 20% (excluding the reference list) will be reviewed manually. High similarity without proper quotation marks will be treated as potential plagiarism.

CW02 Group Project

How are groups formed?

You form your own group of three to four students. Groups must be registered on the VLE by the end of Week 5 (Friday, 18 October 2024, 17:00). Any student not in a registered group by this date will be assigned to a group by the Module Coordinator.

What if a group member stops contributing?

Raise this with Dr Obi as early as possible and no later than Week 12. Do not wait until the submission deadline. Bring any evidence you have of the imbalance. Options depend on the circumstances and how early you raise it.

What must we include in the CW02 submission?

Three things: (1) a PDF technical report, (2) a working code repository on GitHub or GitLab shared with the Module Coordinator, and (3) a ten-minute demo video uploaded to the VLE. All three parts are required.

Can we use AI/ML libraries like scikit-learn or TensorFlow?

Yes. Standard Python AI/ML libraries are expected. You must acknowledge any external code, tutorials, or open-source material you used in your report.

Does every group member get the same mark?

Usually yes. If there is clear evidence of unequal contribution, the Module Coordinator may hold a short individual viva and adjust marks accordingly.

Examinations

When is the EX01 examination?

EX01 takes place during the examination period (6–17 January 2025). The exact date and time are published on the official SEU Examination Timetable. Check the university website or your student portal; the chatbot cannot confirm your individual slot.

How long is the examination?

Two hours.

What topics does the exam cover?

All content from Weeks 1 to 12 (see the module handbook for the full schedule). Past papers are available on the VLE under Exam Resources.

What if I miss the exam due to illness?

Contact the Departmental Student Office within three working days of the exam date. If approved, you may be awarded a sit in the next available sitting. Do not contact the Module Coordinator about missed exams — this is handled by the Student Office.

Module Content and Support

Where can I find lecture slides and lab materials?

All lecture slides are posted on the VLE within 24 hours of each session. Lab materials and Google Colab notebooks are also available under CSC302 → Lab Sessions on VLE.

What programming language is used?

All labs and CW02 must use Python. The recommended environment for lab exercises is Google Colab, which is free and browser-based.

Can I use ChatGPT or other AI tools in my coursework?

For CW01, AI tools may be used only for initial brainstorming. Any AI-assisted content must be declared in an appendix. For CW02, AI coding tools like GitHub Copilot may be used but must be declared in the report. When in doubt, ask Dr Obi in writing before you submit.

When are office hours?

Dr Amaka Obi holds office hours on Tuesdays 10:00–12:00 and Thursdays 14:00–16:00 in Block C, Room 214. You can also email a.obi@seu.edu.ng to arrange a specific time.

When will I receive feedback on CW01 and CW02?

Written feedback for both will be returned within 15 working days of each submission deadline, available on the VLE. To discuss your feedback in person, request a meeting with Dr Obi within two weeks of receiving your marks.

Is this chatbot giving official advice?

This chatbot is a prototype built as part of a student dissertation project. It answers questions based on official CSC302 documents. It is not an official university system, and its answers should not be relied upon for high-stakes decisions. Always verify important matters with Dr Obi, the Student Office, or the official university website.

Project Scope Statement

AI-Powered Student Support Chatbot — CSC302 — South-East University

This document defines the scope of the chatbot artefact built as part of the dissertation: Design and Evaluation of an AI-Powered Student Support Chatbot Using Large Language Models and Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It records the decisions made about what the system will and will not do, which institution and module it is built around, and what documents form its Knowledge Base.

The Institution and Module

Detail

Decision

University name

South-East University (fictitious — no real institutional data used)

Department

Department of Computer Science

Module

CSC302: Artificial Intelligence & Applications

Level

Year 3 Undergraduate (Level 6)

Module Coordinator (fictional)

Dr Amaka Obi

Academic Year

2024/2025



What the Chatbot Will Answer

The chatbot is designed to answer student questions grounded strictly in the eight official CSC302 documents listed in the Knowledge Base register. It will answer questions about:

•  Assessment deadlines (CW01, CW02, EX01)

•  Word limits, file formats, and submission naming conventions

•  How to submit work via the VLE

•  Late submission penalties

•  The extension request process and what qualifies

•  Marking criteria and rubric details

•  Module content and the weekly schedule

•  Contact details and office hours

•  Examination format and content coverage

•  Group project rules for CW02

•  Academic integrity rules and AI tool use policies






What the Chatbot Will Not Answer

The following are explicitly out of scope:

•  Individual student marks, grades, or attendance records (the system has no access to these)

•  Binding decisions on extension eligibility — students are directed to the Student Office

•  Legal, medical, or personal counselling advice

•  Speculation about likely examination questions

•  Anything not addressed in the KB documents

•  Individual exam timetable slots — students are directed to the official timetable
